one_line_takeaway: RAGDEFENDER filters RAG-retrieved passages using training-free clustering/concentration heuristics over TF-IDF terms and embedding similarity, cutting attack success rate against knowledge-corruption/data-poisoning attacks (e.g., 0.89->0.02 for Gemini on NQ at 4x) far below RobustRAG and Discern-and-Answer while eliminating their LLM-inference, fine-tuning, and GPU-memory overhead.
key_finding: On NQ at a 4x adversarial ratio against Gemini, RAGDEFENDER reduces ASR from 0.89 (no defense) to 0.02, versus 0.69 for RobustRAG and 0.24 for Discern-and-Answer. Across all three attack types (PoisonedRAG, GARAG, Tan et al.) on NQ at 4x, RAGDEFENDER limits ASR to 0.03 and achieves accuracy of 0.73/0.75/0.74, versus RobustRAG's ASR of 0.84/0.55/0.54 (accuracy 0.18/0.11/0.18) and Discern-and-Answer's ASR of 0.31/0.30/0.28 (accuracy 0.25/0.25/0.24). On MS MARCO across three retrievers (Contriever, DPR, ANCE) with GPT-4o, average ASR is as low as 0.02-0.04. Efficiency: on NQ at 1x, RAGDEFENDER averages <$0.01 cost and 0.774s per iteration versus RobustRAG's $17.85 average cost and 9.552s (12.36x speedup over RobustRAG, 1.53x over Discern-and-Answer, per the abstract); GPT-4o costs $59.00 under RobustRAG versus <$0.01 under RAGDEFENDER. GPU memory: RAGDEFENDER uses no GPU memory for fine-tuning or inference, versus RobustRAG's 14.7-33.6GB and Discern-and-Answer's 41.8GB (fine-tuning)/4.7GB (inference). Under adaptive evasion (attacker minimizing cosine similarity among injected passages at 2x ratio on MS MARCO), ASR still drops from 0.97 to 0.15, and to as low as 0.05 in the strongest reported adaptive setting, versus 0.76 (RobustRAG) and 0.42 (Discern-and-Answer). The paper's own diagnostic (Section 7) reports adversarial passages have average cosine similarity 0.976 vs. 0.309 for benign passages on MS MARCO, supporting the clustering rationale; mis-partitioning (grouping a legitimate passage as adversarial) occurs in only 0.54% of cases on NQ at 1x; adversarial-passage detection rate is 0.94 on NQ at 4x (estimated Nadv averaging 21.4 vs. 20 actual); and golden-passage retention accuracy under no attack is 97% on NQ, with Gemini showing only a 2% accuracy drop under benign (unpoisoned) conditions when the defense is applied.
core_contribution: The paper introduces RAGDEFENDER, a post-retrieval, training-free defense against knowledge corruption (data poisoning) attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. It is a two-stage pipeline: (1) a grouping stage that estimates the number of adversarial passages Nadv among the retrieved set, using either clustering-based grouping (hierarchical agglomerative clustering over TF-IDF top-term membership, for single-hop QA) or concentration-based grouping (mean/median pairwise cosine-similarity 'concentration factors' over embeddings, for multi-hop QA); and (2) an identification stage that ranks passages by a frequency score fi computed from their appearance in the top cosine-similarity pairs (weighted by similarity^p, p=2) and removes the top-Nadv ranked passages as adversarial before passing the remainder to the generator. The method requires no additional LLM inference, fine-tuning, or GPU usage, and is claimed to be architecture-agnostic (retriever/generator independent).
threat_model: An attacker selects target questions and a desired wrong answer per question, then injects M adversarial passages p~1...p~M into the external knowledge database D such that each is semantically relevant enough to the query to be retrieved (prioritized over benign passages in the paper's worst-case assumption) while embedding attacker-chosen misleading content, aiming to make the generator output the attacker-specified answer. At least one benign passage is guaranteed to still be retrieved. The defender is assumed unaware of which passages (if any) are poisoned and must recover generation quality close to the unpoisoned case using only the retrieved set, without knowledge of ground truth. This is squarely the RAG data-poisoning / knowledge-corruption threat model used by PoisonedRAG-style attacks, distinct from retrieval-poisoning (backdoor-triggered retriever manipulation) and prompt-manipulation attacks, which the paper explicitly scopes out (Figure 1 attack surfaces A/B/C; this work addresses only A).
limitations: The paper's own Section 7 (Discussion and Limitations) states: (1) effectiveness on other corpus types, including multimodal or multilingual datasets, remains untested; (2) performance may become inconsistent when the number of retrieved passages k exceeds 10, though the authors note this falls outside RAG's typical optimal k range (3-5); (3) there is no formal theoretical guarantee that adversarial passages will always form dense embedding clusters -- the method rests on an empirical regularity, not a provable property, and the authors flag stronger adaptive strategies as an open problem for future work. Additionally (not stated by the authors as a limitation but evident from the threat model), the defense assumes adversarial passages are semantically concentrated/co-similar; attacks producing genuinely diverse, mutually dissimilar poisoned passages (rather than the worst-case-prioritized-retrieval assumption used here) are not evaluated as a distinct threat category beyond the adaptive-evasion experiments already covered.

quantitative_results: NQ, 4x ratio, Gemini: ASR 0.89 (no defense) -> 0.02 (RAGDEFENDER) vs 0.69 (RobustRAG) vs 0.24 (Discern-and-Answer)

NQ, 4x ratio, across PoisonedRAG/GARAG/Tan-et-al: RAGDEFENDER ASR 0.03, accuracy 0.73/0.75/0.74; RobustRAG ASR 0.84/0.55/0.54, accuracy 0.18/0.11/0.18; Discern-and-Answer ASR 0.31/0.30/0.28, accuracy 0.25/0.25/0.24
MS MARCO, GPT-4o, 1x ratio, all retrievers: RAGDEFENDER ASR ~0.02 average vs RobustRAG 0.78 vs Discern-and-Answer 0.46
NQ, 1x ratio, average across models: RAGDEFENDER cost <$0.01, speed 0.774s/iteration vs RobustRAG cost $17.85 average, speed 9.552s/iteration (12.36x faster)
GPU memory (NQ, 1x): RAGDEFENDER 0 MB (no GPU usage) vs RobustRAG 14,732-33,634 MB vs Discern-and-Answer 41,836 MB (fine-tuning) / 4,742 MB (inference)
Adaptive evasion (MS MARCO, 2x, cosine-minimizing attacker): ASR 0.97 (no defense) -> 0.15 (RAGDEFENDER), best case 0.05, vs 0.76 (RobustRAG) and 0.42 (Discern-and-Answer)
BlendedRAG/REPLUG/SELF-RAG integration at 6x ratio: largest ASR drop for SELF-RAG, 0.73 -> 0.05 (68% relative reduction), accuracy 0.07 -> 0.45
Section 7 diagnostic: average cosine similarity 0.976 among adversarial passages vs 0.309 among benign passages (MS MARCO)
Mis-partitioning rate 0.54% (NQ, 1x); adversarial-passage detection rate 0.94 (NQ, 4x, estimated Nadv 21.4 vs actual 20); golden-passage retention accuracy 97% (NQ, no attack)
